text: This video demonstrates the technique of sphincteroplasty.
The video shows the loss of the perineal body and a visibly short anogenital distance.
The surgeon observes the anal sphincter, noting its minimal bulk and reduced muscle mass.
A semicircular incision is marked at the periphery of the pigmented skin near the edge of the external anal sphincter.
The surgeon infuses local anesthetic into the surgical area surrounding the anal sphincter, preparing the site for sphincteroplasty.
The surgeon incises the skin along the previously marked semicircular line at the periphery of the pigmented area near the external sphincter.
A self-retaining lone style retractor is positioned to maintain exposure of the surgical field around the anal sphincter.
The surgeon explores the space between the reflections of Collie's fascia of the anal canal and the vagina, carefully examining the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon separates the rectocele from the vaginal wall, revealing the distinct tissue layers between these anatomical structures.
Care is required to prevent breaching either structure.
A swab is packed into the space between the anal canal and vagina, which has been previously explored.
The surgeon mobilizes the left half of the external sphincter, carefully manipulating the muscle tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon mobilizes the right half of the external sphincter, mirroring the procedure performed on the left side.
The surgeon further mobilizes tissue into the left ischiorectal fat, continuing the dissection process in the anal region.
The surgeon mobilizes the transversus perianae muscle, carefully manipulating the tissue in the perianal region.
The surgeon mobilizes the right side of the external sphincter, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon palpates the external sphincter to confirm anatomical landmarks, ensuring accurate identification of the structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully navigates the ischiorectal fossa, ensuring the pudendal nerve and vessels remain undamaged during the procedure.
The surgeon placates the rectocele using PDS sutures after achieving sufficient mobilisation of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs an overlap repair of the external sphincter, aligning and suturing the muscle ends to restore sphincter continuity.
The surgeon repairs the mobilized transversus perianae muscle, ensuring proper alignment and tension within the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the retractor and inserts a drain into the surgical site, ensuring proper placement within the anal region.
The surgeon repairs the posterior fornix of the vagina, ensuring the anatomical structure is restored to maintain sexual function.
The surgeon closes the incision and examines the external sphincter to ensure it remains intact and undisturbed.
The result is a longer anogenital distance with improved pelvic floor strength.
